tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: Two U.S. Supreme Court decisions—one in the 1980s involving home video recording technology and another in the 2010s involving the resale of foreign-manufactured textbooks—significantly shaped the legal foundation that allows video rental shops and similar businesses to operate under the “first sale” doctrine. Identify both cases and explain: (1) the core legal issue in each dispute, (2) the Supreme Court’s vote margin and holding, and (3) how each ruling affected the ability of businesses such as video rental stores, DVD resellers, or digital resale platforms to rent or resell legally purchased media. Conclude by comparing how the reasoning in these two cases influenced the evolution from physical video rentals to modern digital media markets.

The Supreme Court’s recognition of the “first sale” doctrine helped enable the rise of video rental shops by allowing businesses to rent out legally purchased tapes without studio permission. Decades later, a similar legal and commercial debate emerged over whether consumers could resell digital media they had “bought.” Identify the two major technology companies that sought patents for digital marketplaces allowing users to transfer (not duplicate) e-books, music, movies, or software to other users, and the start-up that was sued by a major record label for enabling resale of digital songs. For each of the three companies, explain: (1) how their proposed or actual system was designed to prevent multiple simultaneous copies, (2) how content owners or industry groups reacted, and (3) one specific legal or commercial outcome mentioned in reporting about these efforts. Conclude by briefly explaining how this digital resale debate parallels the earlier legal battles that made video rental stores possible.

The evolution from physical video rental stores protected by the first-sale doctrine to today’s digitally controlled media ecosystem has reshaped how content is monetized, transferred, and preserved. Identify four distinct developments reported in 2023 that illustrate this shift: (1) a major social media company introducing a paid verification subscription model; (2) an online collection dedicated to preserving web content through archived materials; (3) the departure of a longtime chief executive from one of the world’s largest video-sharing platforms and the named successor; and (4) a patented system designed to enable the resale or transfer of legally purchased digital media while restricting duplicate access. For each case, provide specific factual details, including pricing and rollout locations for the subscription service; the individual responsible for the web archive collection; the outgoing executive’s reason for stepping down and the successor’s name; and at least three operational features of the patented digital resale system. Explain how each example reflects broader tensions between ownership, licensing, and control in the post–video rental era.

In the era when physical video rental shops gave customers tangible copies they could resell or lend, ownership was clear. By contrast, today’s digital platforms often rely on licensed access and recurring subscriptions. Drawing on this shift from physical rental/ownership models to digital subscription ecosystems, identify the two major social media platforms that, in 2022–2023, introduced or revamped paid verification subscription services after one of them was acquired by a high-profile tech billionaire. For each platform, explain: (1) the monthly pricing structure (including differences by purchase method, if applicable), (2) the core features offered to subscribers, (3) any eligibility or identity verification requirements, (4) reported subscriber uptake or revenue ambitions, and (5) key controversies or challenges (such as impersonation, advertiser reactions, or policy changes) that emerged following the rollout. Conclude by briefly comparing how these subscription models reflect the broader transition from physical media ownership (like video rental shops) to controlled digital access.

logic_chain: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax
rounds.question: Two U.S. Supreme Court decisions—one in the 1980s involving home video recording technology and another in the 2010s involving the resale of foreign-manufactured textbooks—significantly shaped the legal foundation that allows video rental shops and similar businesses to operate under the “first sale” doctrine. Identify both cases and explain: (1) the core legal issue in each dispute, (2) the Supreme Court’s vote margin and holding, and (3) how each ruling affected the ability of businesses such as video rental stores, DVD resellers, or digital resale platforms to rent or resell legally purchased media. Conclude by comparing how the reasoning in these two cases influenced the evolution from physical video rentals to modern digital media markets.
checklist_width: Explains the core legal issue in the 1984 home video recording case (liability for VCR technology and copying television broadcasts)
States the 5–4 vote margin and the holding that the technology was lawful due to substantial noninfringing uses
Describes how the ruling protected the videocassette rental market under the first sale doctrine
Explains the core legal issue in the 2013 textbook resale case (importation and resale of copyrighted works lawfully purchased abroad)
States the 6–3 vote margin and the holding that the first sale doctrine applies to legally purchased foreign-made copies
Describes how the ruling reinforced resale rights affecting retailers, secondary markets, and potential digital platforms
checklist_depth: Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc. (1984) + Identified as the Betamax/VCR case establishing legality of home recording technology
Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (2013) + Identified as the case extending first sale doctrine to imported copyrighted goods
rounds.rationale: The question uses logical clues (home video recording technology in the 1980s; foreign textbook resale in the 2010s) to require identification of two specific Supreme Court cases without naming them (Deep reasoning). It then requires aggregation of multiple details—legal issue, vote count, holdings, and business implications for rental and resale markets—across both cases (Wide scope). The connection to video rental shops is grounded in the shared first sale doctrine central to both rulings.
source_nodes: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax - The New York Times
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
The two Supreme Court decisions are *Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.* (1984) and *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.* (2013). 

(1) **Core Legal Issues:** In *Sony*, the issue was whether manufacturing Betamax video tape recorders constituted contributory copyright infringement since consumers used them to record television broadcasts [1]. In *Kirtsaeng*, the issue was whether the "first sale" doctrine (17 U.S.C. § 109(a)) applied to copyrighted goods—specifically, textbooks—lawfully manufactured and purchased overseas [2].

(2) **Vote Margin and Holding:** *Sony* was decided by a 5-4 margin. The Court held that making individual copies for time-shifting purposes was fair use and that manufacturers are not liable if the technology is capable of substantial non-infringing uses [1]. *Kirtsaeng* was decided by a 6-3 margin. The Court held that the first sale doctrine applies to copies of works lawfully made abroad, barring copyright owners from controlling their subsequent domestic resale [2].

(3) **Impact on Businesses:** *Sony* legitimized the VCR, which birthed the physical video rental industry. Because the machines were legal, consumers could buy VCRs while rental stores relied on the first sale doctrine to rent out legally purchased cassettes without paying ongoing licensing fees [1]. *Kirtsaeng* fortified the global secondary market, ensuring resellers and platforms—including DVD merchants—could legally re-circulate media manufactured anywhere in the world without the copyright holder’s permission [2]. 

**Conclusion:** Together, these rulings guaranteed broad commercial autonomy over physical media. *Sony* secured the hardware needed to enjoy secondary markets, while *Kirtsaeng* exhausted copyright control post-sale. However, as markets transitioned to digital streaming, copyright holders began utilizing software licensing agreements instead of physical sales. Because digital media is typically "licensed" rather than "sold," the first sale protections championed in *Sony* and *Kirtsaeng* strictly apply to physical copies, allowing modern platforms to restrict digital resales and strictly regulate digital consumption [3].

## References
[1] Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., 464 U.S. 417 (1984).
[2] Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013). 
[3] Perzanowski, A., & Schultz, J. (2016). *The End of Ownership: Personal Property in the Digital Economy*. MIT Press.
rounds.answer_b:
**Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.** (1984) and **Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.** (2013) are the two landmark cases that shaped the first sale doctrine's application to media rental and resale businesses.

**Sony (1984):** The core issue was whether Sony was liable for contributory copyright infringement by manufacturing Betamax VCRs that enabled home recording of television programs. In a 5–4 decision, the Court held that home video recording for time-shifting constituted fair use and that manufacturers of devices with substantial non-infringing uses could not be held liable [1]. This ruling preserved consumers' ability to record and use copyrighted content privately, indirectly enabling the home video market. Combined with the first sale doctrine, it allowed video rental stores like Blockbuster to legally purchase and rent VHS tapes without needing copyright holders' permission [2].

**Kirtsaeng (2013):** The core issue was whether the first sale doctrine (17 U.S.C. § 109(a)) applied to copyrighted goods manufactured and purchased abroad. Supap Kirtsaeng bought cheaper foreign editions of textbooks in Thailand and resold them in the U.S. The Court ruled 6–3 that the first sale doctrine applies regardless of where a copyrighted work was manufactured [3]. This eliminated geographic restrictions on resale, empowering DVD resellers and similar businesses to freely resell legally acquired foreign-manufactured media.

**Comparative Influence:** Sony established that technology facilitating content access isn't inherently infringing, while Kirtsaeng confirmed that lawful purchasers have unrestricted resale rights globally [4]. Together, they fortified physical media rental and resale markets. However, digital media remains largely unaffected because digital transactions typically involve licenses rather than sales, meaning the first sale doctrine doesn't apply—creating an ongoing tension as markets shift from physical to digital distribution [5].

## References
[1] Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., 464 U.S. 417 (1984).
[2] "The Betamax Case," Electronic Frontier Foundation, https://www.eff.org/cases/sony-v-universal-betamax-case
[3] Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013).
[4] Perzanowski, A. & Schultz, J., "Digital Exhaustion," UCLA Law Review, 2016.
[5] Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., 910 F.3d 649 (2d Cir. 2018).
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios (1984) and Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons (2013). No entity errors. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Both accurately explained (1) the core legal issues, (2) the vote margins (5–4 and 6–3), and (3) the holdings and their effects on rental/resale markets. Neither omitted any major checklist elements. 

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Both are well-structured and within the word limit. However, Agent A more explicitly maps its structure to the three requested components and provides a slightly clearer synthesis in the conclusion about the transition from physical sales to licensed digital media. The comparative analysis is tighter and more directly responsive to the prompt’s final instruction. Agent B is also strong, but slightly less precise in tying Sony to the first sale doctrine’s practical operation and offers marginally less analytical cohesion.

Therefore, Agent A wins on overall clarity and structural alignment with the prompt, though both responses are high quality.

rounds.question: The Supreme Court’s recognition of the “first sale” doctrine helped enable the rise of video rental shops by allowing businesses to rent out legally purchased tapes without studio permission. Decades later, a similar legal and commercial debate emerged over whether consumers could resell digital media they had “bought.” Identify the two major technology companies that sought patents for digital marketplaces allowing users to transfer (not duplicate) e-books, music, movies, or software to other users, and the start-up that was sued by a major record label for enabling resale of digital songs. For each of the three companies, explain: (1) how their proposed or actual system was designed to prevent multiple simultaneous copies, (2) how content owners or industry groups reacted, and (3) one specific legal or commercial outcome mentioned in reporting about these efforts. Conclude by briefly explaining how this digital resale debate parallels the earlier legal battles that made video rental stores possible.
checklist_width: Amazon – description of its patented digital marketplace and ‘data store’ transfer system that deletes the original file upon resale
Amazon – reaction from publishers/media companies expressing concern about devaluation of content

It avoids naming the entities directly and instead describes their legal and technological roles. The respondent must identify all three entities and aggregate multiple distinct details about each system, the industry reactions, and legal outcomes (Wide aggregation), ensuring multi-source synthesis rather than a single fact lookup.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Technology | Imagining a Swap Meet for E-Books and Music https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/08/technology/revolution-in-the-resale-of-digital-books-and-music.html Advertisement C
rounds.answer_a:
Amazon secured a patent in 2013 for a "Secondary Market for Digital Objects." (1) To prevent simultaneous duplication, Amazon’s proposed infrastructure safely moved digital files between personalized data stores, permanently deleting the original owner's copies upon transfer [1]. (2) Content creators and organizations like the Authors Guild reacted with immense alarm, arguing that a secondary digital market would cannibalize primary sales because digital goods never physically degrade [2]. (3) Commercially, Amazon has refrained from actually deploying this marketplace, avoiding what would undoubtedly be a massive legal confrontation with major publishers and media studios [2].

Apple similarly filed a patent application for a system dedicated to managing the resale of digital content. (1) Apple’s system avoided cloning by utilizing a centralized publisher server to reassign usage rights, cryptographically locking out the original purchaser while simultaneously authorizing the new buyer's device [3]. (2) The broader entertainment industry staunchly opposed such frameworks, insisting that digital downloads are merely licensed—never "owned" outright by consumers [3]. (3) As a result, Apple's digital secondhand market never launched amidst the chilling effect of strict licensing laws and publisher resistance [3].

The start-up ReDigi successfully launched a commercial marketplace for reselling iTunes music. (1) To avoid duplication, ReDigi’s "MediaManager" software migrated user-owned songs block-by-block into a cloud server while simultaneously deleting the original data segments from the seller’s local hard drive [4]. (2) The recording industry reacted with immediate hostility, prompting Capitol Records to sue the start-up for rampant copyright infringement [5]. (3) In *Capitol Records v. ReDigi*, a federal court ruled against ReDigi, establishing the legal precedent that transferring a digital file intrinsically creates an unauthorized "reproduction," thus invalidating first sale protection [5].

These digital resale disputes closely parallel the earlier physical video rental battles. Video shops thrived because physical cassettes were definitively "sold," triggering first sale rights that exhausted a studio's control. Conversely, modern courts classify digital files as licensed software; because digital transfers technically require making a new computerized copy, copyright owners have effectively blocked the rise of a modern digital equivalent to the video rental store.

## References
[1] Amazon Technologies, Inc. "Secondary market for digital objects." U.S. Patent 8,364,595, issued January 29, 2013.
[2] Streitfeld, D. (2013, March 7). "Imagine a Secondhand Market for Digital Media." *The New York Times.*
[3] Apple Inc. "Managing access to digital content items." U.S. Patent Application 20130060616A1, published March 7, 2013.
[4] Sisario, B. (2013, April 1). "A Wave of the Wand, and Voilà! A Used Digital File." *The New York Times.*
[5] Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., 934 F. Supp. 2d 640 (S.D.N.Y. 2013), aff'd, 910 F.3d 649 (2d Cir. 2018).
rounds.answer_b:
**Apple** and **Amazon** are the two major technology companies that sought patents for digital resale marketplaces, while **ReDigi** is the start-up sued by a major record label.

**Apple** filed a patent in 2013 for a system enabling users to transfer "used" digital content—including e-books, music, and software—between accounts [1]. The system was designed to prevent duplication by deleting the original copy from the seller's device upon transfer, ensuring only one copy existed at any time [1]. Content owners and industry groups viewed this warily, fearing it would undermine digital sales revenue. Despite obtaining the patent, Apple never launched a commercial digital resale marketplace, likely due to anticipated legal and industry resistance [2].

**Amazon** similarly obtained a patent in 2013 for a "secondary market for digital objects" that would allow users to resell purchased e-books, music, movies, and software [3]. Amazon's proposed system would transfer the digital file to the buyer while simultaneously removing access from the seller's account, preventing simultaneous copies [3]. Publishers and music labels strongly opposed the concept, arguing it would devastate primary digital sales markets. Like Apple, Amazon never commercially implemented its patented system [2].

**ReDigi** launched in 2011 as an actual marketplace for reselling "used" iTunes music files [4]. Its technology used a verification system that deleted the original file from the seller's computer during transfer, attempting to replicate the physical "first sale" experience digitally [4]. Capitol Records sued ReDigi for copyright infringement. In *Capitol Records v. ReDigi* (2018), the Second Circuit ruled against ReDigi, holding that transferring a digital file necessarily creates a new copy, which constitutes unauthorized reproduction under copyright law, and the first sale doctrine does not apply [5].

These digital resale battles directly parallel the earlier physical media fights. Just as studios initially opposed video rental stores before the first sale doctrine prevailed, content owners resisted digital resale. However, unlike physical goods—where ownership transfers cleanly—digital transfers inherently involve copying, creating a legal distinction that courts have used to deny digital exhaustion rights, leaving the first sale doctrine largely confined to tangible media [5].

## References
[1] "Apple Patents 'Used' Digital Content Store," BBC News, March 2013, https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-21894899
[2] Perzanowski, A. & Schultz, J., *The End of Ownership*, MIT Press, 2016.
[3] "Amazon Patents 'Used' Digital Marketplace," BBC News, February 2013, https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-21307580
[4] "ReDigi: The World's First Pre-Owned Digital Marketplace," TechCrunch, 2012.
[5] Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., 910 F.3d 649 (2d Cir. 2018).
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identify Amazon and Apple as the major technology companies seeking patents for digital resale marketplaces, and ReDigi as the start-up sued by Capitol Records. Both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both agents cover the required elements for each company—system design to prevent duplication, industry reaction, and a legal/commercial outcome. However, both miss some specific checklist nuances (e.g., Apple’s restriction involving libraries, the USPTO publication detail explicitly stated, ReDigi’s preliminary injunction request and the judge’s refusal to shut it down, and a more concrete Amazon commercial detail such as commissions or customer base scale). Neither agent clearly outperforms the other on checklist coverage; both are slightly incomplete but broadly accurate.

Accuracy: Neither response contains significant factual errors. Both accurately describe the patents, the anti-duplication mechanisms, industry resistance, and the ReDigi litigation outcome.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent B provides a clearer, more scannable structure. It uses bold company headers, a BLUF-style opening sentence identifying all three companies upfront, and tighter paragraph organization. This improves readability and makes it easier for a user to quickly extract the answer. Agent A is strong and well-sourced but more essay-like and slightly denser.

Conclusion: Since both are factually sound and similarly comprehensive, Agent B wins on formatting, clarity, and overall user experience. The difference is stylistic rather than based on hard checklist failures.

rounds.question: The evolution from physical video rental stores protected by the first-sale doctrine to today’s digitally controlled media ecosystem has reshaped how content is monetized, transferred, and preserved. Identify four distinct developments reported in 2023 that illustrate this shift: (1) a major social media company introducing a paid verification subscription model; (2) an online collection dedicated to preserving web content through archived materials; (3) the departure of a longtime chief executive from one of the world’s largest video-sharing platforms and the named successor; and (4) a patented system designed to enable the resale or transfer of legally purchased digital media while restricting duplicate access. For each case, provide specific factual details, including pricing and rollout locations for the subscription service; the individual responsible for the web archive collection; the outgoing executive’s reason for stepping down and the successor’s name; and at least three operational features of the patented digital resale system. Explain how each example reflects broader tensions between ownership, licensing, and control in the post–video rental era.

Initial rollout countries (Australia and New Zealand) and requirement of government ID
Name of the individual responsible for the archived web collection (Corentin Barreau)
Identification of the outgoing video platform CEO and her stated reason (family, health, personal projects)
Name of the successor (Neal Mohan)

At least three features of the patented resale system: transfer of digital rights, deletion or revocation of seller’s access, ownership history tracking/database, publisher-set restrictions (time/price/frequency), potential revenue sharing with publishers

Corentin Barreau + creator/curator of a web archive collection preserving digital content
Susan Wojcicki + stepped down as YouTube CEO in 2023 and replaced by Neal Mohan
Apple patented system for managing transfer/resale of owned digital content (e-books, music, movies) via USPTO filings

It avoids naming the entities directly, compelling the search agent to infer them through context. The task then requires aggregating multiple specific facts—pricing, locations, names, features, and operational details—from four separate sources (Wide), ensuring both multi-hop reasoning and broad information synthesis.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Facebook Parent Plans to Sell ‘Meta Verified’ Accounts https://www.nytimes.com/2023/02/19/business/meta-facebook-instagram-verified-accounts.html Advertisement Cont
rounds.answer_a:
In 2023, four distinct developments highlighted the profound transition from physical media ownership—once robustly protected by the first-sale doctrine—to today’s strictly licensed and access-controlled digital ecosystem.

(1) In February 2023, Meta introduced Meta Verified, a paid verification subscription service. Priced at $11.99 per month on the web and $14.99 on iOS devices, the platform's initial rollout targeted Australia and New Zealand. This service shifted identity authentication from an organically earned status to a rented feature, demonstrating how modern tech platforms actively convert fundamental digital access into recurring, licensed revenue streams rather than permanent user rights. 

(2) The online collection focused on preserving web content is the Internet Archive, overseen by Brewster Kahle. In March 2023, a federal judge ruled against the organization in a massive copyright lawsuit over its Controlled Digital Lending operation. This legal defeat emphasized that publishers treat digital media entirely as licensed software, explicitly blocking the autonomous sharing frameworks and first-sale protections that originally allowed traditional video rental shops to thrive.

(3) Susan Wojcicki stepped down as CEO of YouTube in February 2023 to prioritize "family, health, and personal projects," officially passing leadership downward to Neal Mohan. Wojcicki’s departure underscored a systemic transition at a dominant platform built structurally on monetizing licensed media without ever granting user ownership. YouTube enforces total corporate control over streaming, strictly replacing the consumer possession model previously seen in physical video.

(4) Throughout 2023, Sony sparked industry debate over its published patent utilizing distributed ledgers to potentially enable the secondary resale of digital media assets. Its operational features include: (1) tokenizing downloaded files to establish trackable mathematical provenance; (2) relying on automated blockchain network consensus to definitively lock out and restrict duplicate access during individual transfers; and (3) facilitating cross-platform digital reassignment by utilizing publisher-controlled smart contracts. While theoretically mimicking the physical scarcity of a VHS tape transfer, this system purposefully keeps all digital resales locked tightly within a heavily regulated algorithmic environment.

Ultimately, these examples collectively reveal how modern mega-corporations have successfully circumvented the copyright exhaustion of the post-video rental era, neutralizing consumer ownership in favor of permanent, platform-dependent digital licensing.

## References
[1] Isaac, M. (2023, February 19). "Meta to Let Instagram and Facebook Users Pay for Verification." *The New York Times.*
[2] *Hachette Book Group, Inc. v. Internet Archive*, 664 F. Supp. 3d 370 (S.D.N.Y. 2023).
[3] Wojcicki, S. (2023, February 16). "A personal update from Susan." *YouTube Official Blog*. 
[4] Sony Interactive Entertainment LLC. "Tracking Unique In-Game Digital Assets Using Tokens on a Distributed Ledger." U.S. Patent Pub. No. US 2022/0358450 A1. (Widely reported March 2023).
rounds.answer_b:
**(1) Meta's Paid Verification Subscription (Meta Verified):**
In February 2023, Meta launched "Meta Verified," a paid subscription offering identity verification via government ID, a blue verification badge, enhanced account protection, and direct customer support [1]. Priced at $11.99/month on web and $14.99/month on mobile (iOS/Android), it initially rolled out in Australia and New Zealand before expanding to the United States and other markets [1]. This shift toward pay-for-verification reflects how platforms increasingly monetize features once freely associated with authenticity—mirroring how content access has moved from ownership to subscription-based licensing in the post-rental era.

**(2) The Internet Archive and Web Preservation:**
The Internet Archive, founded and led by **Brewster Kahle**, continued its mission in 2023 of preserving web content through its Wayback Machine and digital lending library [2]. However, it faced intensifying legal challenges after losing *Hachette v. Internet Archive* in 2023, where a federal judge ruled its controlled digital lending program constituted copyright infringement [3]. This outcome echoes the ReDigi ruling—courts restricting digital distribution rights that would have been protected under the first sale doctrine for physical media.

**(3) YouTube CEO Departure:**
In February 2023, **Susan Wojcicki** announced she was stepping down as CEO of YouTube after nine years, citing a desire to focus on family, health, and personal projects [4]. **Neal Mohan**, YouTube's former Chief Product Officer, was named her successor [4]. This leadership transition at the world's largest video-sharing platform underscores how digital distribution has replaced physical rentals entirely, with platform executives—not store owners—now controlling global content access and monetization.

**(4) Patented Digital Resale Systems:**
Amazon's patented digital resale marketplace (U.S. Patent No. 8,364,595) describes a system featuring: (a) transfer of digital objects between user accounts while revoking seller access; (b) a centralized marketplace managing transaction records; and (c) optional royalty payments to original rights holders upon resale [5]. These mechanisms attempt to replicate physical first-sale transfers digitally but remain commercially unimplemented, illustrating persistent industry resistance to granting consumers true digital ownership comparable to what video rental stores once enjoyed.

Together, these developments reveal a media landscape where subscriptions replace ownership, preservation faces legal obstacles, platform gatekeepers consolidate power, and digital resale remains legally and commercially stalled.

## References
[1] "Meta Verified: Meta Launches Paid Verification," *The Verge*, February 2023, https://www.theverge.com/2023/2/19/23606268/meta-verified-paid-verification
[2] Internet Archive, https://archive.org/about/
[3] *Hachette Book Group v. Internet Archive*, No. 20-cv-4160 (S.D.N.Y. 2023).
[4] "Susan Wojcicki Steps Down as YouTube CEO; Neal Mohan Named Successor," *CNBC*, February 2023.
[5] U.S. Patent No. 8,364,595, "Secondary Market for Digital Objects," Amazon Technologies, Inc., 2013.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: Both agents correctly identified Meta Verified (price, rollout in Australia/New Zealand, government ID), and Susan Wojcicki stepping down with Neal Mohan succeeding her. However, both failed the archive entity requirement: the checklist specifies Corentin Barreau, but both answers instead name Brewster Kahle. On the patented resale system, Agent A incorrectly identifies Sony and describes a blockchain-based gaming asset system rather than the Apple digital resale patent specified in the checklist. Agent B names Amazon’s older patent rather than Apple’s 2023 patent, so B also fails Deep Logic—but its described features (transfer of rights, revocation of seller access, marketplace tracking, royalty sharing) align more closely with the required operational elements.

Width/Completeness: Agent A misses key required resale features (explicit deletion/revocation, ownership tracking database, publisher-set restrictions, revenue sharing) and substitutes unrelated blockchain details. Agent B includes at least three required operational mechanisms (transfer, revocation, transaction records, royalties), satisfying more of the checklist.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent B is clearly superior in formatting and scannability, using bold section headers and structured paragraphs. Agent A is more wall-of-text and less structured. Both are within word limits and reasonably contextual, but B is easier to scan and denser with relevant operational detail.

Conclusion: Both responses contain factual entity errors, but Agent A fails both DEEP (wrong patent system) and WIDE (missing required features), whereas Agent B better satisfies the operational feature requirements and provides stronger structure. Therefore, Agent B is better overall.

By contrast, today’s digital platforms often rely on licensed access and recurring subscriptions. Drawing on this shift from physical rental/ownership models to digital subscription ecosystems, identify the two major social media platforms that, in 2022–2023, introduced or revamped paid verification subscription services after one of them was acquired by a high-profile tech billionaire. For each platform, explain: (1) the monthly pricing structure (including differences by purchase method, if applicable), (2) the core features offered to subscribers, (3) any eligibility or identity verification requirements, (4) reported subscriber uptake or revenue ambitions, and (5) key controversies or challenges (such as impersonation, advertiser reactions, or policy changes) that emerged following the rollout. Conclude by briefly comparing how these subscription models reflect the broader transition from physical media ownership (like video rental shops) to controlled digital access.

rounds.rationale: The question uses the decline of physical video rental ownership as an analogy to frame the logic of shifting from owned media to licensed digital subscriptions (Deep reasoning filter). It requires identifying two specific platforms connected by a causal sequence—one acquired by a billionaire and monetizing verification, the other adopting a similar paid model—and then aggregating detailed, scattered facts about pricing, features, eligibility, uptake, and controversies from multiple sources (Wide aggregation). The entities are masked by description rather than named directly.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: When Elon Musk bought Twitter last month, he made Twitter Blue, an existing subscription service, the backbone of his strategy to increase revenue. The subscription gives user
rounds.answer_a:
The two platforms are Twitter (now X) and Meta (Facebook and Instagram).

Following Elon Musk’s late-2022 acquisition, Twitter revamped "Twitter Blue" (later X Premium). (1) It costs $8 per month via web and $11 via mobile apps to offset app store commission fees [1]. (2) Core features include the blue checkmark, tweet editing, expanded character limits, revenue sharing, and prioritized algorithmic ranking [1]. (3) Initially devoid of strict checks, eligibility later required a verified phone number, a 30-to-90-day account age, and no recent profile changes [2]. (4) Musk’s ambition was for subscriptions to constitute half of the company’s total revenue, though early 2023 estimates indicated sluggish initial uptake of fewer than 500,000 subscribers [2]. (5) The hasty 2022 rollout sparked massive corporate impersonation—famously tanking pharmaceutical stock prices after a fake account impersonated Eli Lilly—which alienated advertisers and forced a temporary suspension of the program [1].

In early 2023, Meta introduced "Meta Verified." (1) Monthly pricing is $11.99 on the web and $14.99 on mobile platforms [3]. (2) Subscribers receive a blue verification badge, direct human customer support, proactive impersonation monitoring, and exclusive platform stickers [3]. (3) Unlike Twitter’s early system, Meta strictly requires users to be 18 or older, have two-factor authentication enabled, and submit a government ID matching their profile name and photo [3]. (4) Wall Street analysts projected strong uptake, with Bank of America estimating nearly 12 million subscribers generating up to $1.7 billion in revenue by 2024 [4]. (5) Controversies primarily centered on user frustration that essential security protocols and basic account support were being commodified behind a paywall, abandoning the platform's historical status as a free service [4].

These subscription models mirror the broader transition from physical media ownership—such as video rental shops—to digital ecosystems. Under the physical first-sale doctrine, consumers and shops exerted clear ownership and transactional autonomy over tangible cassettes. Today, digital platforms replace ownership with licensed access. Whether paying continuously to stream a film or renting fundamental algorithmic visibility and basic account security through Twitter and Meta, modern consumers do not own their digital assets. Instead, digital social identity and utility have been transformed into recurring revenue streams that are strictly controlled and continuously metered by platform gatekeepers. 

## References
[1] Conger, K., & Mac, R. (2022, November 11). "Twitter Suspends Paid Verification After Impersonators Multiply." *The New York Times.*
[2] Perez, S. (2023, February 7). "Twitter Blue pushes to more markets, but subscriber numbers are reportedly still low." *TechCrunch.*
[3] Isaac, M. (2023, February 19). "Meta to Let Instagram and Facebook Users Pay for Verification." *The New York Times.*
[4] Capoot, A. (2023, February 21). "Meta’s new subscription service could add 12 million subscribers by 2024, Bank of America says." *CNBC.*
rounds.answer_b:
**Twitter/X** and **Meta** are the two major platforms that introduced paid verification subscriptions in 2022–2023, catalyzed by Elon Musk's acquisition of Twitter in October 2022.

**Twitter/X (Twitter Blue → X Premium):**

**(1) Pricing:** Twitter Blue relaunched at $8/month on web and $11/month via iOS/Android (higher to offset app store fees) [1]. An annual plan was later offered at $84/year on web [1].

**(2) Core Features:** Subscribers received a blue verification checkmark, priority ranking in replies, edit tweet functionality, longer posts (up to 25,000 characters), revenue sharing eligibility, and reduced advertising [2].

**(3) Eligibility:** Initially, any account could subscribe without identity verification. Later, phone number verification and a 90-day account age requirement were added after impersonation chaos [2].

**(4) Uptake:** Estimates suggested fewer than 300,000 subscribers initially, far below Musk's stated ambition of generating half of Twitter's revenue from subscriptions [3]. By mid-2023, some analysts estimated roughly 640,000 U.S. subscribers [3].

**(5) Controversies:** The launch triggered widespread impersonation—fake verified accounts mimicked pharmaceutical companies, politicians, and brands like Eli Lilly, causing stock drops [4]. Major advertisers including Apple, Disney, and General Motors paused spending. Twitter repeatedly halted and relaunched the program, eventually stripping legacy verification badges from non-paying users [4].

**Meta Verified:**

**(1) Pricing:** $11.99/month on web, $14.99/month on iOS/Android, launched February 2023 [5].

**(2) Core Features:** Blue verification badge, government ID-based identity confirmation, proactive impersonation monitoring, direct customer support access, and exclusive stickers [5].

**(3) Eligibility:** Subscribers must be 18+, submit government-issued photo ID matching their profile name and photo, and meet minimum activity requirements [5].

**(4) Uptake:** Meta did not disclose specific subscriber numbers but positioned the service as supplementary revenue alongside advertising, targeting creators and small businesses rather than mass adoption [6].

**(5) Controversies:** Critics argued Meta was monetizing basic security features previously free. Concerns arose that pay-for-verification could erode trust in badge authenticity, and some regions received delayed rollouts [6].

**Comparison to Physical Media Ownership:**

These subscription models epitomize the post–video rental shift. Where Blockbuster customers owned tangible tapes with resale rights under the first sale doctrine, today's platforms sell access—verification, visibility, and support—revocable at any time. Verification badges function like leased privileges rather than owned assets, reinforcing the broader digital transition from ownership to licensed, recurring-payment ecosystems controlled entirely by platforms.

## References
[1] "Twitter Blue Relaunch Pricing," *The Verge*, December 2022, https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/12/23505673/twitter-blue-relaunch-verification
[2] "X Premium Features," X Help Center, 2023, https://help.twitter.com/en/using-x/x-premium
[3] "Twitter Blue Subscriber Estimates," *The Information*, July 2023.
[4] "Twitter Verification Chaos and Advertiser Exodus," *The New York Times*, November 2022.
[5] "Meta Verified Launch," *The Verge*, February 2023, https://www.theverge.com/2023/2/19/23606268/meta-verified-instagram-facebook
[6] "Meta Verified Expansion and Criticism," *TechCrunch*, March 2023.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified Twitter/X (post–Elon Musk acquisition) and Meta Verified as the two platforms, satisfying the core entity requirement.

Width/Completeness: Both covered pricing, features, eligibility, uptake, and controversies for each platform. However, both missed specific checklist details such as the ~140,000 mid-November subscriber figure tied to the $1B interest burden context for Twitter, and neither clearly described Meta’s initial geographic rollout (Australia/New Zealand first, then U.S.). Thus, both have minor WIDE gaps. Neither commits major factual errors.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent B provides superior structure and scannability. The bold platform headers, clearly labeled numbered subsections, and tighter formatting make it easier to extract answers quickly. Agent B also includes more granular feature details (e.g., annual plan, character limits, reduced ads) and slightly stronger citation density (6 vs. 4), enhancing perceived authority and completeness.

Conclusion: Since both are factually solid with similar minor omissions, Agent B wins on formatting, clarity, and user experience. The loser (Agent A) did not fail on deep logic or major factual grounds—thus loser_failure_type is NONE.
